ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for bresaola-and-cheese platter; closest defensible anchor is charcuterie food product (FOODON_03315116). Platter composition includes cured beef (bresaola), feta cheese, black olives, and bread.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — meat-based mixed platter; FoodEx2 level-1 anchor for composite meat dishes used as closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for bresaola-and-cheese platter. A00LC (composite meat dish) used as the nearest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to this specific platter composition.

preparation_method: no cooking required for primary components

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard platter serving of approximately 300 g, typically shared as a starter or light meal for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Beef, cured, dried; Cheese, feta; Olives, ripe, canned; Bread, white, commercially prepared — composite weighted estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a bresaola-and-cheese platter. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of four USDA SR Legacy entries: bresaola/dried cured beef (~30% weight), feta cheese (~25% weight), black olives (~15% weight), and white bread (~30% weight). The input record's per-100g values (energy 216.67 kcal, protein 11.67 g, fat 13.33 g, carbohydrate 12.67 g, sodium 600 mg) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the composite estimate is insufficiently precise for individual reporting.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using white bread GI (75) weighted against low-GI components (feta, bresaola, olives GI ~0–15) at approximate platter proportions.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a bresaola-and-cheese platter. GI estimated as a weighted composite: white bread component (GI 75, ~30% of platter by weight) combined with negligible-GI components — bresaola (GI ~0), feta cheese (GI ~0), black olives (GI ~0) — yields a blended platter GI of approximately 52 (low category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (GI 52 × available carbohydrate 12.67 g per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 9.9 g GL. This is a low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g from cured bresaola, feta cheese, and brined black olives — all high-sodium preserved products
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (50–75 g) and avoid adding any additional salt, as the sodium content of 600 mg per 100 g substantially exceeds the recommended per-meal sodium threshold of 400–600 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (1.0 g per 100 g) and moderate carbohydrate (12.67 g per 100 g) with the primary carbohydrate load coming from the bread component; protein (11.67 g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this platter in moderate portions (150–200 g), prioritising the bresaola, feta, and olive components while limiting bread to one small slice, as the bread is the primary glycaemic driver and the overall platter GI is estimated at 52 (low category).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g per 100 g (exceeds 5 g per 100 g CVD threshold) combined with sodium 600 mg per 100 g and trans fat 0.17 g per 100 g from processed components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this platter to occasional small portions (75–100 g) due to the combination of high saturated fat from feta cheese, high sodium from cured bresaola and brined olives, and the presence of trans fat; substituting feta with fresh mozzarella and reducing the bresaola portion would meaningfully lower the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g per 100 g exceeds the 5 g RA threshold; bresaola as cured red beef contributes arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); sodium 600 mg per 100 g worsens joint tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this platter; if consumed, replace bresaola with smoked salmon to shift the fatty acid profile toward anti-inflammatory omega-3, and request reduced feta and olive quantities to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bresaola and Cheese Platter is a cold assembled appetiser platter featuring bresaola — an Italian air-dried, salt-cured lean beef with a deep ruby colour, tender texture, and mildly savoury, slightly gamey flavour — served alongside crumbly, tangy feta cheese, brined black olives, and freshly baked homemade bread. The platter requires no hot cooking; components are sliced and arranged cold. Bresaola originates from the Valtellina region of northern Italy and is produced by curing whole beef topside with salt, herbs, and spices before air-drying for two to three months. In Singapore restaurant contexts, this dish is served as a European-style antipasto starter or light sharing plate. Nutritionally, the platter provides 216.67 kcal per 100 g with 11.67 g protein, 13.33 g fat (of which 6.67 g is saturated fat from feta), and 12.67 g carbohydrate primarily from the bread component. Sodium is high at 600 mg per 100 g due to the cured meat, brined olives, and preserved cheese. The platter is clinically notable for its high sodium and saturated fat content, making it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients in regular portions, while the low sugar content and moderate GI (~52) make it more manageable for Type 2 Diabetes patients who limit the bread component. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified due to the cured pork-adjacent processing environment of some bresaola products, though beef-based bresaola itself is halal by ingredient.
common_user_queries: Is bresaola and cheese platter suitable for diabetics?
High sodium foods to avoid with hypertension

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA SR Legacy composite of four component foods (bresaola/dried cured beef, feta cheese, black olives, white bread) as no single approved-source entry exists for this specific platter. The input record's values are broadly consistent with the composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous weighted estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the platter composition is well-defined.
notes: 1. Nutrient values derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dried cured beef, feta cheese, black olives, and white bread at approximate platter proportions (30/25/15/30 by weight). No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite platter are not available with sufficient precision from USDA FoodData Central component entries to report reliably at the composite level as of 2024. 3. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as Middle Eastern cuisine with ethnic tags for Shawarma and Kebab — these have been corrected to Italian/European. 4. The input record's halal dietary tag has been removed pending verification; bresaola is beef-based but curing facility halal certification is not confirmed. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous weighted estimate; no direct GI measurement for this platter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. All core macros (protein_g 11.67, fat_g 13.33, carbohydrate_g 12.67) are non-zero and supported by the USDA composite estimate.
